title: Unauthenticated proxy to Qiwa government API (/api/qiwa)
description: The REST route apps/web/app/api/qiwa/route.ts exposes GET, POST and PUT handlers that acquire a Qiwa OAuth token and proxy the request straight to the Qiwa government API (api.qiwa.sa / sandbox). No auth() session check, no role check, no tenant scoping and no Zod validation are performed. The path is also NOT included in middleware.ts config.matcher, so the global rate-limit and same-origin CSRF checks never run for it. The request body is forwarded verbatim to the Qiwa endpoint (JSON.stringify(body)) and the employee id comes from an unvalidated ?id= query param. A properly gated tRPC equivalent (qiwaRouter, requireRole(...QIWA_MANAGE_ROLES)) already exists, so this is an unprotected parallel door to the same integration.
expectedBehavior: Every call to a government-integration endpoint must require an authenticated session, enforce the qiwa manage roles, be tenant-scoped, validate the payload with the existing qiwaEmployeeSchema, and be covered by rate limiting.
actualBehavior: Any unauthenticated caller can POST/PUT arbitrary employee data to Qiwa or GET employee records, using the server's own Qiwa credentials, with no session, role, tenant, validation or rate limit.

evidence.snippet: export const config = { matcher: [ "/api/auth/callback/credentials", ... "/api/trpc/:path*", ... ] };  // /api/qiwa is absent -> no middleware, no rate-limit, no CSRF

reproduction: curl -X POST https://<host>/api/qiwa -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"fullName":"x"}'  (no cookie/session) -> request is proxied to Qiwa and the raw Qiwa response is returned.
technicalRisk: Server-side abuse of stored Qiwa credentials; unvalidated payload passed to an external government API; SSRF-adjacent proxy behaviour; internal error text leaked to the caller.
businessRisk: Fraudulent creation/modification of employee/contract records in the Saudi government Qiwa system under the tenant's identity; regulatory and legal exposure; credential abuse.
recommendation: Add auth() + role check (QIWA_MANAGE_ROLES) at the top of every handler, resolve tenant from the session, validate the body with qiwaEmployeeSchema/qiwaContractSchema, add /api/qiwa to middleware.matcher for rate-limit + CSRF, and return generic error messages. Preferably retire this REST route in favour of the already-gated tRPC qiwaRouter.
workaround: Block /api/qiwa at the edge/WAF or delete the route until authentication is added.

title: Unauthenticated cross-tenant data exposure (/api/auth/check-tenants)
category: Broken Access Control / Excessive Data Exposure
description: apps/web/app/api/auth/check-tenants/route.ts GET returns the 10 most recently created tenants (id, companyName, crNumber, nitaqatActivity, planTier, regulatoryContext, schemaName) and the 10 most recent users (id, email, name, role, tenantId) with NO authentication. This is the same registry data that the tRPC procedure auth.tenantsList deliberately gates behind a PLATFORM_ADMIN_EMAILS allowlist (SEC-002) and which even omits schemaName. The route is not in middleware.matcher, so it is not even rate limited.
expectedBehavior: Cross-tenant registry data (customer company names, Saudi CR numbers, internal Postgres schema names, and user emails/roles) must only be readable by an authenticated platform operator, matching the auth.tenantsList gate.
actualBehavior: Anyone on the internet can enumerate customer companies, CR numbers, internal schema names, and user emails/roles across ALL tenants by requesting one URL.

evidence.snippet: tenantsList: protectedProcedure.query(async ({ ctx }) => { const allowedOperators = (process.env.PLATFORM_ADMIN_EMAILS ?? "").split(",")...; if (!email || !allowedOperators.includes(email)) throw new TRPCError({ code: "FORBIDDEN" }); // gated equivalent

reproduction: curl https://<host>/api/auth/check-tenants  -> JSON dump of recent tenants (incl crNumber + schemaName) and users (incl email + role).
technicalRisk: Discloses internal Postgres schema-per-tenant names (aids further attacks), user emails and roles, and tenant identifiers with zero authentication.
businessRisk: PII leak (emails), disclosure of customer identities and Saudi commercial-registration (CR) numbers across the whole platform; multi-tenant confidentiality breach; PDPL exposure.
recommendation: Delete this diagnostic route, or gate it behind the same PLATFORM_ADMIN_EMAILS allowlist used by auth.tenantsList and add it to middleware.matcher. Never return schemaName to any client.
workaround: Remove the route file immediately; it is redundant with auth.tenantsList.

title: Custom /api/auth/login is a brute-force oracle (no rate limit, no Zod)
description: apps/web/app/api/auth/login/route.ts performs a raw bcrypt password comparison and returns the user's id/email/role/tenantId. It only checks that email and password are present (no Zod, no email-format or length bounds). The middleware rate limiter and per-account lockout are keyed on /api/auth/callback/credentials (the NextAuth path); this custom /api/auth/login route is NOT in middleware.matcher, so it has no rate limiting and no account lockout - a standalone unthrottled password-checking oracle.
expectedBehavior: All credential-verification endpoints should be rate limited per IP/account, enforce account lockout, and validate input. Ideally there should be a single authentication path (NextAuth).
actualBehavior: Attackers can brute-force passwords against /api/auth/login at full speed with no throttling or lockout; generic error text is good but the endpoint still confirms valid credentials and returns role/tenantId.

evidence.snippet: const isLoginAttempt = pathname.startsWith("/api/auth/callback/credentials");  // does NOT cover /api/auth/login

reproduction: Loop POST /api/auth/login with a fixed email and a password list; observe no 429 throttling and no lockout after many attempts.
technicalRisk: Unlimited offline-speed password guessing against live accounts; no lockout integration.
businessRisk: Account takeover of HR/super_admin accounts leading to full tenant payroll/PII compromise.
recommendation: Either remove this route in favour of NextAuth, or add it to middleware.matcher with the login rate-limit bucket, wire it to the same per-account lockout used in authorize(), and validate the body with a Zod schema (emailSchema + bounded password).
workaround: Add /api/auth/login to middleware.matcher and reuse the login:<ip> rate-limit key.

title: employee.getById / employee.list over-select salary + PII and lack object-level scoping
description: employee.getById and employee.list run on companyProcedure, which only excludes the employee and candidate roles - recruiter and department_manager are admitted. Both return the whole employees row (no column projection), including plaintext salaryBasic/salaryHousing/salaryTransport and the *_enc PII columns (iqamaNumberEnc, passportNumberEnc, bankIbanEnc). Unlike employee.list (which department-scopes a department_manager), employee.getById performs NO department scoping, so a department_manager or recruiter can fetch ANY employee's full record - including salary - by iterating UUIDs. This overlaps RBAC-004 (recruiter/department_manager must not see salary) but is reported here as a data-layer over-selection + missing object-level authorization issue.
expectedBehavior: Reads should project only the columns the caller is entitled to (salary and *_enc PII stripped for recruiter/department_manager) and getById should enforce the same department scoping as list.
actualBehavior: getById returns the entire employee row (salary + encrypted PII + payslips relation) to any company role with no department scoping; a recruiter sees salary they are explicitly not permitted to see.

evidence.snippet:
getById: companyProcedure.input(z.string().uuid()).query(async ({ ctx, input }) => {
    return await ctx.db.query.employees.findFirst({ where: eq(schema.tenant.employees.id, input), with: { department: true, manager: true, documents: true, leaveRequests: true, payslips: true } }); });  // no dept scope, whole row

export const companyProcedure = protectedProcedure.use(({ ctx, next }) => { const role = ctx.user.role; if (role === "employee" || role === "candidate") throw new TRPCError({ code: "FORBIDDEN" }); return next({ ctx }); });  // recruiter + department_manager admitted

reproduction: As a recruiter or department_manager, call trpc employee.getById with any employee UUID -> response includes salaryBasic/salaryHousing/salaryTransport and *_enc columns; the target need not be in the manager's department.
technicalRisk: Data-layer over-selection of sensitive columns; object-level authorization missing on getById (IDOR across departments).
businessRisk: Salary confidentiality breach and PII exposure to roles that must not see them; PDPL/compliance impact.
recommendation: Add a column allowlist (Drizzle columns projection) that strips salary and *_enc fields for non-payroll roles, and apply getManagedDepartmentIds scoping to employee.getById as is already done in employee.list. Coordinate with the RBAC owner (RBAC-004).
workaround: Restrict getById/list to the salary-authorized roles until per-column projection is added.

title: File upload storage path traversal via unsanitized category field
description: In apps/web/app/api/upload/route.ts the object-storage key is built as `${tenantId}/${category}/${timestamp}_${safeFileName}`. The file name is sanitized (safeFileName), but the client-supplied `category` string is used raw. A value such as `../<otherTenantId>` would resolve the stored object outside the caller's tenant prefix within the Supabase bucket, breaking tenant isolation of stored files.
expectedBehavior: The storage path segment derived from category must be validated against an allowlist (or sanitized the same way the filename is) so it cannot contain path separators or traversal sequences.
actualBehavior: category is concatenated into the storage path without sanitization; `../` or slash characters allow escaping the tenant folder.
reproduction: POST multipart to /api/upload with field category set to `../<victimTenantId>/docs` and observe the object written under the victim tenant's prefix.
technicalRisk: Cross-tenant file placement/overwrite (x-upsert:true is set); breaks tenant storage isolation.
businessRisk: Tenant data-isolation violation for uploaded documents; potential overwrite of another tenant's stored files.
recommendation: Validate category against a fixed enum (e.g. policies, contracts, ids) or apply the same [^a-zA-Z0-9._-] sanitization used for the filename; reject null/empty category.
workaround: Enforce an allowlist of category values server-side.

title: Internal error strings leaked to clients (qiwa, health)
description: Several REST handlers return raw error text to the caller. /api/qiwa returns error.message (which can include upstream Qiwa/DB detail) and /api/health returns String(err) on failure. In contrast the tRPC error formatter and the login/signup/reset routes correctly return generic messages. This leaks internal detail (hostnames, driver errors) to unauthenticated clients.
expectedBehavior: Client-facing errors should be generic; detailed errors logged server-side only (as done in /api/auth/login).
actualBehavior: Raw error.message / String(err) is serialized into the HTTP response body.

evidence.snippet: } catch (error) { return NextResponse.json({ error: error instanceof Error ? error.message : String(error) }, { status: 500 }); }
} catch (err) { return NextResponse.json({ error: String(err) }, { status: 500 }); }

reproduction: Trigger a DB/upstream error against /api/health or /api/qiwa and inspect the response body for internal error text.
technicalRisk: Leaks internal implementation/infrastructure detail useful for reconnaissance.
businessRisk: Assists attacker reconnaissance; minor confidentiality impact.
recommendation: Replace error bodies with a generic message and log the detail via console.error server-side.
workaround: Wrap responses with a generic error string.

title: In-memory rate limiter is per-instance and unbounded; CSRF skipped when Origin absent
description: middleware.ts implements rate limiting with a module-level Map (rateLimitMap). On serverless/edge (Vercel) each instance has its own Map, so limits are per-instance and largely ineffective under horizontal scaling; the Map also never evicts expired entries (unbounded memory growth). Additionally, the same-origin CSRF check only rejects when an Origin header is present and mismatched - requests with no Origin header (e.g. non-browser clients) bypass the check.
expectedBehavior: Rate limiting should use a shared store (Redis) so limits hold across instances, evict expired keys, and CSRF/same-origin protection should fail-closed for state-changing requests lacking a trustworthy Origin.
actualBehavior: Limits reset per instance and per deploy; Map grows unbounded; missing Origin header skips the CSRF check.

evidence.snippet: const rateLimitMap = new Map<string, { count: number; resetAt: number }>();  // per-instance, no eviction
if (origin) { try { if (new URL(origin).host !== host) return 403; } catch { return 403; } }  // no origin -> check skipped

reproduction: Send state-changing POSTs to /api/upload without an Origin header (curl) -> CSRF check is bypassed; distribute login attempts so they land on different lambda instances -> per-IP limit is not enforced globally.
technicalRisk: Rate limiting ineffective at scale; memory leak; CSRF bypass for non-browser clients.
businessRisk: Weakened brute-force/DoS/CSRF protections on sensitive endpoints.
recommendation: Back rate limiting with Redis (already a dependency via BullMQ) keyed by IP+route; evict expired keys; require a valid Origin/Referer (fail-closed) for state-changing custom routes, or rely on NextAuth CSRF tokens.
workaround: Add an edge/WAF rate limit; require Origin header on state-changing routes.

title: Raw SQL string interpolation in demo seed endpoint
description: apps/web/app/api/seed/demo-data/route.ts builds INSERT/UPDATE statements by string-interpolating values (emp.id, t.id, balance, year, department ids) directly into SQL passed to adminDb.execute(). The interpolated values are trusted (UUIDs/numbers read from the DB, not user input) and the endpoint is guarded by a NODE_ENV!=production check plus MIGRATION_TOKEN, so exploitability is low - but the pattern is an injection-prone anti-pattern and the only string-concatenated SQL in the codebase alongside the migrate routes.
expectedBehavior: All queries should use Drizzle's parameterized builder or sql`` with bound parameters, never raw string interpolation - per the project's 'never raw SQL' convention (CLAUDE.md).
actualBehavior: Values are concatenated into raw SQL strings via .replace() and template literals.

evidence.snippet: await adminDb.execute(`INSERT INTO ${schema}.leave_balances (id, employee_id, leave_type_id, balance, year) VALUES (gen_random_uuid(), '${emp.id}', '${t.id}', ${balance}, ${year})`);

reproduction: Not remotely exploitable in production (blocked by NODE_ENV guard + token); flagged as an insecure pattern / defense-in-depth gap.
technicalRisk: If any interpolated source ever becomes attacker-influenced, this becomes SQL injection.
businessRisk: Low while values are DB-derived and endpoint is demo-only.
recommendation: Delete the demo seed/migrate endpoints after the demo (as their own comments state), or rewrite using parameterized Drizzle inserts.
workaround: Ensure NODE_ENV=production in all live environments (currently the case) and remove the endpoints post-demo.

title: Retention update schemas allow client to set employeeId/status via input.data spread
description: The retention router applies updates with `.set({ ...input.data, updatedAt })` across ~40 procedures. Several update schemas in packages/validators/src/retention.ts include mutable identity/state fields such as employeeId (optional) and status (optional). Because the whole validated object is spread into .set(), a caller can reassign a record to a different employeeId or force a status transition directly, bypassing the intended workflow. Department write-scoping helpers exist (scoping.ts assertManagesGoal/Review/...) and procedures are capability-gated, which limits blast radius, but the schemas should not expose employeeId/status as freely-settable update fields. Marked suspected pending confirmation that each update procedure calls the corresponding assert* helper.
expectedBehavior: Update DTOs should allowlist only the fields a caller may change; immutable ownership keys (employeeId) and workflow state (status) should be excluded from generic update schemas or handled via dedicated, guarded transitions.
actualBehavior: employeeId and status are present in update schemas and spread straight into .set().

evidence.snippet: .set({ ...input.data, updatedAt: new Date() }).where(eq(...id, input.id))  // spreads employeeId/status from client

reproduction: Call a retention update mutation with data.employeeId set to another employee's UUID (or data.status set to a terminal state) and observe the record reassigned/transitioned.
technicalRisk: Record reassignment across employees; unguarded status transitions.
businessRisk: Performance/retention data integrity (goals, reviews, PIPs) can be silently reassigned or force-completed.
recommendation: Remove employeeId (and status where it should be a guarded transition) from the *Update schemas, or explicitly pick allowed fields before .set(). Ensure every update calls the matching assertManages* scoping helper.
workaround: Add explicit field picking in each retention update before writing.

title: AI chat forwards client-supplied model string to the LLM provider
description: ai.chat.send passes input.model (validated only as z.string().min(1).optional()) straight to getLlmClient().complete({ model: input.model }). If packages/llm does not allowlist the model, a client could request an arbitrary or more expensive model, driving up cost. The message array is well-bounded (max 20 messages, 8000 chars each) and temperature is capped, which limits other abuse.
expectedBehavior: The model override should be constrained to a server-side allowlist of approved models.
actualBehavior: Any non-empty model string is forwarded to the provider.

evidence.snippet: model: z.string().min(1).optional(),  // no enum / allowlist
const res = await client.complete({ system: systemPrompt, messages: ..., model: input.model });

reproduction: Call ai.chat.send with model set to an expensive/unexpected model id and observe whether packages/llm rejects it.
technicalRisk: Cost inflation / provider abuse if model is unrestricted.
recommendation: Validate model against z.enum([...approvedModels]) or resolve it server-side per topic; ignore client model unless it is in the allowlist.
workaround: Drop input.model server-side and pick the model per provider.

title: company/profile PATCH and company/invite POST parse body without Zod
description: Both handlers destructure request.json() manually instead of validating with a Zod schema. company/profile PATCH writes industry/companySize/website with no length/format bounds (unbounded strings). company/invite POST validates email only via `email?.trim()` (no email-format check) though it does allowlist the role. Both are auth+role gated so the risk is limited to authenticated tenant admins, but this deviates from the project's Zod-everywhere convention and permits unbounded/malformed data.
expectedBehavior: All request bodies validated with a Zod schema (bounded strings, emailSchema).
actualBehavior: Manual destructuring; no schema; unbounded strings; email format unchecked on invite.

evidence.snippet:
const body = await request.json();
  const { industry, companySize, website } = body;  // no Zod, unbounded

const { email, role = "employee" } = body;
  if (!email?.trim()) return 400;  // no email-format validation

reproduction: PATCH /api/company/profile with a multi-megabyte website string, or POST /api/company/invite with email='not-an-email' -> accepted.
technicalRisk: Unbounded string storage; malformed invite emails.
recommendation: Introduce Zod schemas (bounded strings + emailSchema) and safeParse the body, matching signup/reset routes.
workaround: Add length caps and an email regex check inline.

title: Password-reset link (raw token) logged to server console in all environments
description: request-reset generates a single-use reset token and, because email delivery is still a TODO, logs the full reset URL (including the raw token) via console.info for every request in every environment. Anyone with access to server/log aggregation can read valid reset tokens and take over accounts within the token TTL (1 hour).
expectedBehavior: Reset tokens/links must never be logged; deliver via the email package. Any diagnostic logging should be gated to non-production and must not include the raw token.
actualBehavior: console.info logs email + full reset URL with the raw token unconditionally.

evidence.snippet: console.info("[auth] password reset link generated for", email, resetUrl);  // resetUrl contains rawToken

reproduction: Trigger a reset for a known email and read the reset URL (with token) from server logs; use it to reset the password.
technicalRisk: Valid password-reset tokens exposed in logs.
businessRisk: Account takeover for anyone with log access; PDPL/audit concern.
recommendation: Remove the token from logs; wire up @hrms-app/email delivery. If a dev fallback is needed, gate it to NODE_ENV!=production and log only that a link was generated (no token).
workaround: Delete the console.info line and rely on email delivery.

title: Unauthenticated /api/vitals accepts partially-validated payload (crash + log injection)
description: /api/vitals is unauthenticated (acceptable for a beacon) and validates payload.name against an allowlist, but does not validate that payload.value is a number before calling payload.value.toFixed(2) - a non-numeric value throws outside the try/catch, yielding an unhandled 500. payload.path is logged unsanitized (log-injection vector). Low sensitivity, but input is not fully validated (no Zod).
expectedBehavior: Validate the whole payload with Zod (name enum, value number, bounded path) before use.
actualBehavior: Only name is checked; value type and path content are trusted.

evidence.snippet: if (payload.rating === "poor") { console.warn("[vitals][poor]", payload.name, payload.value.toFixed(2), payload.path ?? ""); }  // value assumed numeric; path unsanitized

reproduction: POST /api/vitals {"name":"LCP","value":"x","rating":"poor"} -> value.toFixed throws -> 500; or set path to a CRLF string to inject log lines.
recommendation: Validate the payload with a Zod schema (value: z.number(), path bounded/stripped of control chars) and wrap logging in the try/catch.
workaround: Coerce/guard value and strip control characters from path.
